one_line_takeaway: AegisAgent is a training-free, three-layer autonomous defense agent (sanitize -> verify consistency -> robust-reason) for LLM-based wearable HAR systems that cuts prompt-injection attack success by ~30 points across five target models and three datasets at ~79ms latency, with its ablation showing the robust-reasoning layer is by far the most load-bearing component.
key_finding: AegisAgent reduces average Attack Success Rate (ASR) by roughly 30 percentage points and achieves ~85% average Detection Accuracy (DA) and ~75% average Semantic Consistency (SC) across the five LLM-HAR systems and four downstream classifiers (Table 1), while adding only 78.6 ms average per-query latency (Table 3) on the RTX 3090 workstation. The ablation study (Fig. 6) shows the Robust Reasoner is the most critical component: removing it crashes DA from the full-system level down to just 7-8%, versus 53-57% without the Input Sanitizer and 46-49% without the Consistency Verifier. The attack-severity analysis (Table 2) establishes a clear hazard hierarchy: text-path attacks are least severe (HS 2.6-2.8, avg 2.70), prompt-path attacks moderate (HS 2.9-3.1, avg 3.00), signal-path attacks more severe (HS 3.5-3.6, avg 3.55), and hybrid/composite attacks most severe (HS 3.8-4.1, avg 3.97) -- confirming the paper's Challenge-3 claim that multi-layer/compound attacks are the most dangerous. In the baseline comparison (Fig. 7/Sec 5.5), AegisAgent (93.0% DA, 56.3% RR, 27.2% ASR) substantially outperforms text-only defense (44.1% DA, 7.9% RR, 74.3% ASR), classical HAR defense (58.7% DA, 15.8% RR, 66.1% ASR), and multimodal detection-only defense (64.9% DA, 21.7% RR, 60.4% ASR).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) a systematic empirical/formal study of prompt injection attacks specific to LLM-based Human Activity Recognition (LLM-HAR) systems, formalizing three attack paths -- signal path (raw IMU perturbation), text path (tampering with the intermediate sensor-to-text description), and prompt path (direct manipulation of the user-visible prompt) -- plus a hybrid/composite attack that combines all three; (2) a catalog of 15 concrete instantiated attacks (detailed in Appendix Table 4, referenced but not reproduced in the extracted pages) spanning these paths; and (3) AegisAgent, an autonomous, training-free, model-agnostic defense agent built as a three-layer pipeline (Input Sanitizer -> Consistency Verifier -> Robust Reasoner) coordinated by a Planning Agent, Memory Hub, and Executor Agent, all backed by a Gemma-2-9B model for embeddings and reasoning. AegisAgent's Memory Hub performs sensor-side sanitization via MAD-weighted normalization (threshold tau_MAD=2.5) and text-side sanitization via an Aho-Corasick lexical filter with a greylist coefficient alpha=0.4; its Consistency Verifier computes semantic conflict scores (threshold tau_c=0.35, escalation threshold tau_sem=0.6) via a Sentence-BERT-style encoder and temporal consistency via FastDTW alignment (conflict flagged at S_cm<0.6); its Robust Reasoner triggers chain-of-thought templates, self-consistency multi-path voting, and a debate-style veto mechanism when thresholds are exceeded.
threat_model: Attackers can manipulate prompts fed to LLM-HAR pipelines at three intervention points: (1) signal path -- perturbing raw IMU signals or their textual surrogates (noise injection, drift, temporal splicing); (2) text path -- tampering with the intermediate sensor-to-text description that feeds the LLM (synonym substitution, paraphrase edits, few-shot poisoning); (3) prompt path -- directly manipulating the full user-visible prompt (task injection, role confusion, chain-of-thought disruption, instruction overwriting). The paper explicitly assumes a white-box setting, justified by the fact that several target LLM-HAR systems (e.g., IMUGPT-2.0, MotionGPT) are open-sourced, giving attackers full access to model architecture, parameters, and weights. The attacker's goal is to make the LLM output align with an injected objective (e.g., mislabeling a fall as normal walking) rather than the true activity, and the paper frames this as safety-critical because misclassification in wearable/HAR contexts (e.g., in eldercare fall detection) can cause physical harm -- motivating their custom Harm Score metric.
limitations: The paper states a white-box threat model, which is a strong assumption that may not hold for closed/proprietary LLM-HAR deployments; several of the target LLM-HAR systems used for evaluation are indeed open-sourced, but generalization to fully black-box commercial systems is not tested. Robust Reasoning is only activated conditionally (~20% activation rate per Table 3's latency breakdown), so its 78.6 ms average latency figure may not represent worst-case overhead when robust reasoning fires on every query. The preliminary case study (Sec 2.2) is conducted only on IMUGPT-2.0 in detail; the paper states 'we observe similar results in other LLM-HARs (results not included in the paper due to space limitation)' -- i.e., some claimed generalization is asserted, not shown, in the paper. All target-model training and inference queries route through a single backbone (Gemma-2-9B) for the agent's own reasoning/embedding, which could itself become an attack surface or single point of failure not analyzed by the paper. No adaptive/adversarial attacker that specifically targets AegisAgent's own detection thresholds (e.g., tau_san=0.75, tau_c=0.35, tau_exec=0.85) is evaluated -- all 15 attacks appear to be static, not adaptively crafted against the defense itself, so robustness to an adaptive attacker who knows AegisAgent's architecture is untested within the reviewed text.

quantitative_results: Average Detection Accuracy (DA) across 5 models/4 classifiers: 85%
Average Attack Success Rate reduction: ~30 percentage points
AegisAgent vs baselines: 93.0% DA, 56.3% RR, 27.2% ASR
Classical HAR defense baseline: 58.7% DA, 15.8% RR, 66.1% ASR
Multimodal detection-only baseline: 64.9% DA, 21.7% RR, 60.4% ASR
Ablation w/o Input Sanitizer: DA drops to 53-57%
Ablation w/o Consistency Verifier: DA drops to 46-49%
Ablation w/o Robust Reasoner: DA drops to 7-8%
Harm Score by category: Text attacks avg 2.70, Prompt attacks avg 3.00, Signal attacks avg 3.55, Hybrid attacks avg 3.97 (1-5 scale)
Total AegisAgent latency: 78.6 ms average per query on 3x RTX 3090 GPU workstation

Preliminary case study on IMUGPT-2.0: unattacked accuracy 92.13%/88.47%/85.26% (USC-HAD/UCI HAR/PAMAP2) drops to 52.67%/47.92%/45.27% after attack, partially recovers to 57.94%/52.72%/49.79% with standard text-only defenses
baselines_compared: Classical HAR defenses (denoising + FGSM/PGD adversarial training)
